narrative:
Short impact summary

The article titled “Super-Spreaders” and Person-to-Person Transmission of Andes Virus in Argentina published in the New England Journal of Medicine has garnered significant academic and public attention, evidenced by 142 citations and mentions on platforms such as Bluesky.

Full narrative

Published in the New England Journal of Medicine in 2020, the article “Super-Spreaders” and Person-to-Person Transmission of Andes Virus in Argentina represents a pivotal contribution to understanding viral transmission dynamics in emerging infectious diseases. With a citation count of 142, the work clearly resonates within academic circles, signaling its importance in the field.

In addition to its scholarly impact, the narrative surrounding this work has found traction in social discourse, particularly on Bluesky, where it has been referenced four times. This highlights its relevance not only in academic discussions but also in public forums, pointing to growing societal interest in the implications of the Andes virus transmission.

The research was backed by notable institutions including the National Institutes of Health and the United States Army Medical Research Institute of Infectious Diseases, showcasing a formidable collaborative effort. The funding from prominent agencies such as the US National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases underscores an institutional investment in the significance of infectious disease research, particularly in relation to viruses with pandemic potential.

The article’s broad reach is further evidenced by its citations stemming from a diverse set of countries, with the United States and Argentina leading the way. This international footprint emphasizes the global relevance of findings related to the Andes virus, supporting its role in the ongoing discourse surrounding emerging infectious diseases and their management in public health contexts.
